attrs.alt: 5-phase ADKAR-based OCM plan for enterprise Claude adoption

content.text: Why Enterprise Claude Adoption Needs an OCM Plan
Deploying Claude across an enterprise is a

, not a software release. Even when the technology works perfectly, adoption fails if employees do not understand why Claude is being introduced, do not see personal benefit, do not know how to use it safely, cannot perform confidently, or revert to old workflows once the launch excitement fades.

The technology risk in enterprise AI adoption is typically lower than the change risk. Most Claude deployments stall not because the model underperforms but because the organisation's people, processes, and culture were not prepared.
The ADKAR Framework for Claude Adoption

The Prosci ADKAR model (Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement) provides a sequenced view of the individual change journey. Each phase has a measurable gate; the next phase should not begin organisation-wide until the gate is met.
Do I know why we're adopting Claude?
Communicate the what, why, and timeline clearly
Rumour fills the vacuum; resistance hardens
Do I want to use Claude?
Show personal benefit; address fears; engage managers
Compliance without adoption; teams use it to tick boxes
Do I know how to use Claude safely and effectively?
Train by role; provide safe-use guidelines; set up support
Misuse, PII errors, low-quality outputs, learned helplessness
Can I actually do it in my day-to-day work?
Coach in context; remove blockers; measure real usage
Skills exist but habits don't form; relapse to old methods
Is using Claude rewarded and sustained?
Celebrate wins; adjust incentives; build community of practice
Adoption spikes at launch then decays within 90 days

The awareness phase answers one question for every employee: why is the organisation adopting Claude, what will change for them, and what will not. Uncertainty fuels resistance; specificity reduces it.
Before launching communications, survey managers and a sample of individual contributors to understand the specific concerns in this organisation. Generic change messaging misses real objections.

# resistance_audit.py — classify employee concerns and generate targeted responses
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ResistanceConcern:
    source_group: str   # "frontline engineers" | "managers" | "legal" | etc.
    concern: str

def analyse_resistance(concerns: list[ResistanceConcern]) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    concern_text = "\n".join(f"[{c.source_group}] {c.concern}" for c in concerns)
    prompt = f"""Analyse these employee resistance concerns about enterprise Claude adoption.

{concern_text}

For each concern:
1. CATEGORY: Job security / Data privacy / Quality / Workflow disruption / Trust / Other
2. VALIDITY: Is this concern legitimate? (Yes / Partially / No)
3. RESPONSE STRATEGY: How should communications address this specifically?

Then:
TOP 3 THEMES across all groups
HIGHEST-RISK GROUPS (most likely to actively resist adoption)
RECOMMENDED COMMUNICATIONS SEQUENCE (which concerns to address first and why)
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

concerns = [
    ResistanceConcern("frontline engineers", "Claude will write code that looks right but has bugs I'm responsible for"),
    ResistanceConcern("frontline engineers", "My job will be automated away in 2 years if I help this succeed"),
    ResistanceConcern("managers", "My team's output quality will drop if they rely on AI without thinking"),
    ResistanceConcern("legal", "Confidential client information might leak into Anthropic's training data"),
    ResistanceConcern("support agents", "I'll be evaluated on AI-generated responses I didn't write"),
    ResistanceConcern("senior engineers", "Junior engineers will use Claude as a crutch and never learn properly"),
    ResistanceConcern("data team", "We don't know what data is being sent to the API and where it's stored"),
]
analysis = analyse_resistance(concerns)
print(analysis)
Awareness creates attention; desire creates motivation. The key message in this phase is
WIIFM — What's In It For Me
. Generic productivity claims ('save 20% of your time') land worse than specific, role-relevant demonstrations of time saved on tasks people actually hate doing.

# wiifm_generator.py — generate role-specific benefit narratives for adoption comms
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RoleProfile:
    title: str
    pain_points: list[str]   # top 3 time-wasting or frustrating tasks
    daily_routine_tasks: list[str]

def generate_wiifm(role: RoleProfile) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    pain = "\n".join(f"- {p}" for p in role.pain_points)
    routine = "\n".join(f"- {r}" for r in role.daily_routine_tasks)
    prompt = f"""Write a compelling, specific WIIFM (What's In It For Me) message
for a {role.title} about Claude adoption.

Their biggest pain points:
{pain}

Their daily routine tasks:
{routine}

Write 3 short paragraphs (150–200 words total):
1. What Claude specifically does for THIS role — concrete, not generic
2. One realistic example scenario where Claude saves meaningful time or improves quality
3. What they do NOT have to worry about (address the top fear for this role)

Tone: peer-to-peer, direct, not corporate. No buzzwords.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

roles = [
    RoleProfile(
        "Software Engineer",
        ["Writing boilerplate code", "Reading unfamiliar codebases", "Writing ticket descriptions after meetings"],
        ["Code reviews", "Debugging", "Sprint ceremonies", "Documentation updates"],
    ),
    RoleProfile(
        "Customer Support Agent",
        ["Searching knowledge base for answers", "Writing follow-up emails", "Escalation writeups"],
        ["Ticket triage", "Customer calls", "Team meetings", "End-of-day reporting"],
    ),
]
for role in roles:
    print(f"\n=== {role.title} ===")
    print(generate_wiifm(role))

Training for Claude adoption differs from standard software training in two ways: the tool is non-deterministic (the same prompt produces different outputs on different runs), and the risks of misuse are less visible (PII leakage, hallucinated facts, biased outputs) than with traditional software errors.

# safe_use_guidelines.py — generate role-specific safe use guidelines
import anthropic

def generate_safe_use_guide(role: str, use_cases: list[str], restricted_data: list[str]) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    use_text  = "\n".join(f"- {u}" for u in use_cases)
    data_text = "\n".join(f"- {d}" for d in restricted_data)
    prompt = f"""Generate practical safe-use guidelines for a {role} using Claude at work.

Approved use cases for this role:
{use_text}

Data types this role MUST NOT include in prompts:
{data_text}

Write guidelines covering:
1. WHAT TO DO (3-4 approved use cases with example prompt patterns)
2. WHAT NOT TO DO (the specific restricted data types and why they're restricted)
3. HOW TO VERIFY (how to check Claude's output before using it — role-specific quality checks)
4. WHAT TO DO WHEN UNSURE (escalation path)

Tone: practical, specific, no legalese. Max 400 words.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

guide = generate_safe_use_guide(
    role="Customer Support Agent",
    use_cases=[
        "Draft email replies to customers (always review before sending)",
        "Summarise ticket history before a call",
        "Suggest knowledge base articles matching customer issue",
    ],
    restricted_data=[
        "Customer names and email addresses",
        "Account numbers or payment information",
        "Internal system passwords or API keys",
        "Legal case details or contracts",
    ],
)
print(guide)
Ability is built through supervised practice in real workflows, not classroom training. Reinforcement prevents the 90-day adoption decay that kills most enterprise tool rollouts.

# adoption_dashboard.py — track adoption metrics across all five ADKAR phases
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AdoptionSnapshot:
    week: int
    awareness_pct: float          # % of staff who know about Claude adoption
    pilot_active_users_pct: float # % of pilot cohort actively using
    training_completion_pct: float
    avg_weekly_queries_per_user: float
    satisfaction_score: float     # 1.0 – 5.0 from pulse survey
    top_blocker: str

ADKAR_TARGETS = {
    "awareness":   0.70,
    "desire":      0.60,
    "knowledge":   0.80,
    "ability":     0.50,   # 50% reporting time saving
    "retention":   0.70,   # 70% still active at 90 days
}

def generate_adoption_report(snapshots: list[AdoptionSnapshot]) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    history = "\n".join(
        f"Week {s.week}: awareness={s.awareness_pct:.0%}, "
        f"active_pilots={s.pilot_active_users_pct:.0%}, "
        f"training={s.training_completion_pct:.0%}, "
        f"queries/user/week={s.avg_weekly_queries_per_user:.1f}, "
        f"CSAT={s.satisfaction_score:.1f}, blocker='{s.top_blocker}'"
        for s in snapshots
    )
    targets_text = "\n".join(f"  {k}: {v:.0%}" for k, v in ADKAR_TARGETS.items())
    prompt = f"""Analyse this Claude adoption programme metrics history.

ADKAR Targets:
{targets_text}

Weekly Snapshots:
{history}

Generate a concise adoption health report:
1. ADKAR PHASE ASSESSMENT — which phases are on track / behind / at risk?
2. TREND ANALYSIS — what is accelerating or decelerating adoption?
3. TOP BLOCKER — what is the single most important thing to fix this week?
4. RECOMMENDED ACTIONS — 2-3 specific actions for the OCM team this week
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

snapshots = [
    AdoptionSnapshot(4,  0.72, 0.00, 0.00, 0.0, 0.0, "No blocker yet — awareness phase"),
    AdoptionSnapshot(8,  0.78, 0.55, 0.20, 2.1, 3.8, "Managers not reinforcing usage"),
    AdoptionSnapshot(12, 0.80, 0.62, 0.65, 3.4, 4.0, "Training content too generic for support team"),
    AdoptionSnapshot(16, 0.82, 0.68, 0.79, 4.2, 4.1, "Prompt quality varies widely across teams"),
    AdoptionSnapshot(20, 0.84, 0.71, 0.85, 5.8, 4.3, "Help desk queue averaging 2-day response"),
]
print(generate_adoption_report(snapshots))
The single biggest predictor of enterprise adoption success is

from the most senior person in the affected business unit. Employees watch their manager: if their manager uses Claude, they will try it. If their manager is sceptical or passive, no amount of training will close the gap.

# sponsor_brief.py — generate a concise sponsor briefing for executive leaders
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SponsorContext:
    sponsor_name: str
    sponsor_title: str
    business_unit: str
    staff_count: int
    key_concerns_from_their_team: list[str]
    business_outcomes_targeted: list[str]
    current_phase: str

def generate_sponsor_brief(ctx: SponsorContext) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    concerns  = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in ctx.key_concerns_from_their_team)
    outcomes  = "\n".join(f"- {o}" for o in ctx.business_outcomes_targeted)
    prompt = f"""Generate a concise sponsor briefing document for {ctx.sponsor_name} ({ctx.sponsor_title}).
They are the executive sponsor for Claude adoption in {ctx.business_unit} ({ctx.staff_count} staff).
Current adoption phase: {ctx.current_phase}.

Key concerns from their team:
{concerns}

Business outcomes targeted:
{outcomes}

The briefing should cover (max 400 words):
1. WHAT ACTIVE SPONSORSHIP LOOKS LIKE for this person specifically
   (e.g. "use Claude in your next leadership meeting", "share a specific prompt that saved you time")
2. THE THREE QUESTIONS YOUR TEAM IS ASKING — and how to answer them
3. WHAT TO WATCH FOR — metrics that will tell you if adoption is working in your unit
4. TWO THINGS TO DO THIS WEEK

Write to the executive, not about them. Direct second-person tone.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

ctx = SponsorContext(
    sponsor_name="Sarah Kim",
    sponsor_title="VP of Engineering",
    business_unit="Platform Engineering",
    staff_count=85,
    key_concerns_from_their_team=[
        "Fear that AI will automate senior engineer roles",
        "Worry about code quality if junior engineers rely on AI",
        "Uncertainty about what data is safe to include in prompts",
    ],
    business_outcomes_targeted=[
        "Reduce PR review cycle time from 3 days to 1 day",
        "Cut boilerplate documentation writing time by 60%",
        "Enable junior engineers to unblock themselves faster on unfamiliar codebases",
    ],
    current_phase="Desire (week 7 of 10)",
)
print(generate_sponsor_brief(ctx))
Use Claude visibly in team meetings
Manager opens a meeting by showing a real prompt they used this week
'Have you tried Claude for X?' as a routine coaching question
Slack shout-out or stand-up mention of a specific time saving
Manager escalates tool-access or training gaps within 48 hours
Set a team usage norm (not mandate)
Team agrees: 'We try Claude before googling for boilerplate'
Measuring OCM Success — Leading and Lagging Indicators

OCM plans fail when they measure only lagging indicators (productivity gains, CSAT) and miss the leading signals (awareness, engagement, training completion) that predict those outcomes weeks earlier.
% of staff who know what Claude is being used for
% of pilot cohort with ≥ 3 Claude interactions/week
% of trained staff passing competency assessment
% of users who still active at day 30 post-training
User satisfaction with Claude at work

# pulse_survey.py — generate phase-appropriate adoption pulse survey questions
import anthropic

def generate_pulse_survey(phase: str, role_groups: list[str]) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    prompt = f"""Generate a 5-question pulse survey for the {phase} phase of enterprise Claude adoption.
Target role groups: {', '.join(role_groups)}.

The survey should:
- Take < 3 minutes to complete
- Mix Likert-scale questions (1-5) with one open-text question
- Focus on leading indicators appropriate to the {phase} phase
- Avoid jargon; write as if the respondent may be sceptical

Output the questions in this format:
Q1. [Question text] (Scale: 1 = Strongly Disagree → 5 = Strongly Agree)
Q2. ...
Q5. [Open text question]

Also include: WHAT TO DO IF AVERAGE SCORE < 3.0 ON Q1-4 (one paragraph of recommended actions).
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Phase-appropriate surveys
for phase in ["Awareness", "Desire", "Reinforcement"]:
    print(f"\n{'='*50}")
    print(f"PULSE SURVEY — {phase} Phase")
    print('='*50)
    print(generate_pulse_survey(phase, ["Software Engineers", "Support Agents", "Data Analysts"]))
Common OCM Failure Modes for AI Adoption
Treating it as a tool launch, not a behaviour change
: sending a 'Claude is now available' email and calling it communications. Employees need repeated, specific, role-relevant engagement — not a single announcement.
: moving straight from awareness ('we're doing this') to training ('here's how') without addressing 'but why should I bother?' Training without desire produces compliant but passive non-users.

: teaching everyone the same prompting techniques regardless of role. A support agent and a software architect use Claude completely differently. One-size training wastes both groups' time and fails to build real ability.
: building a direct-to-employee adoption programme without coaching managers. Employees who see their manager ignoring Claude will infer it is not actually important.
: tracking API calls but not asking whether employees feel confident, are producing better outputs, or have encountered problems they do not know how to report.
: launching strong and then going silent. Adoption decays within 60–90 days without sustained reinforcement. Plan the reinforcement calendar before launch, not after decay sets in.

An OCM plan for enterprise Claude adoption applies the ADKAR framework across five phases: Awareness (weeks 1–4) clears up why the change is happening; Desire (5–10) makes the personal benefit concrete by role; Knowledge (11–18) trains safe and effective use; Ability (19–28) coaches employees in real workflows; and Reinforcement (week 29+) sustains adoption against the 90-day decay curve. Claude accelerates the OCM programme itself — analysing resistance themes, generating role-specific WIIFM narratives, producing safe-use guidelines, and synthesising adoption dashboards into actionable reports. The OCM plan does not succeed because of the tools; it succeeds because it treats adoption as a human behaviour change problem, not a software deployment problem.
